home.seo_title: Learning Brains – Industrial Training with AI | Erasmus+
home.seo_description: Learning Brains is an Erasmus+ KA220-VET project developing AI-powered work-based learning tools to reskill industrial workers in Europe. Discover our results, partners, and free resources.
home.hero_subtitle: Integrated Work-Based Learning Systems for Industrial Reskilling
home.pillars_subtitle: Learning Brains introduces a new paradigm for industrial training by combining three key elements into a unified framework.

about.seo_title: About the Project – Learning Brains Erasmus+
about.seo_description: Discover the goals, focus areas, and target groups of Learning Brains: an Erasmus+ KA220-VET project bridging artificial intelligence and vocational training in the European manufacturing sector.
about.title: About the Project
about.background_text: The rapid evolution of industrial processes, driven by digitalization and automation, is creating an increasing need for workforce reskilling across Europe. Traditional training approaches often fail to address the specific, contextual learning needs of workers who must adapt to new technologies and processes while remaining productive. Learning Brains addresses this challenge by developing integrated work-based learning systems that combine hands-on workplace experience with structured educational approaches.
objectives_list: Develop innovative work-based learning methodologies tailored for industrial reskilling
Create AI-based tools to support personalized learning paths in work environments
Foster cooperation between VET providers and industrial partners across Europe
Produce open educational resources for industrial reskilling accessible to a wide audience
Contribute to the European policy dialogue on modernizing vocational education and training
target_list: VET providers and trainers in industrial sectors
HR managers and training coordinators in industry
Policy makers in vocational education and employment

partners.seo_description: Meet the 6 European organizations from Spain, Italy, Slovakia, Austria, and Portugal collaborating on the Erasmus+ Learning Brains project about AI and industrial vocational training.
partners.label: Transnational Collaboration
partners.title: Our Partners
partners.subtitle: Learning Brains is a transnational partnership bringing together industrial associations, business support agencies, and training experts from across Europe.
descriptions: A leading industrial federation representing over 800 companies in the metal sector, committed to innovation and training.
The service company of Confindustria Veneto, specializing in training, innovation, and organizational development for regional companies.
Representing the interests of the Styrian business community and providing extensive educational and training support services.
Experts in the design and implementation of innovative pedagogical methodologies and digital learning environments.
The oldest specialized non-profit organization for supporting small and medium-sized enterprises in Slovakia.
Dedicated to human resource development and innovative training solutions for the modern job market.

impact.seo_title: Dissemination and Impact – Learning Brains Erasmus+
impact.seo_description: Learn about the dissemination strategy and social impact of Learning Brains: promoting AI literacy, worker adaptability, and industrial reskilling across 5 European countries.
impact.label: Dissemination & Impact
impact.title: Project Influence
impact.subtitle: Understanding how Learning Brains contributes to modernizing European vocational training and industrial reskilling.
impact.strategy_text: Our strategy focuses on reaching key stakeholders across Europe to ensure that project results are widely known and utilized. We use a multi-channel approach including this digital hub, sectoral social media, regional industrial events, and transnational workshops.
impact.impact_text: The Learning Brains project expects to contribute to the modernization of VET systems by providing validated tools for industrial reskilling. Impact will be observed in enhanced adaptability of industrial workers to technological changes and increased efficiency of workplace training methodologies.
impact.note: Note: Project impacts are currently described non-quantitatively as implementation is in its initial phases.

items_list.title: AI-Powered Learning: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Industrial Training
items_list.description: AI-driven learning tools boost knowledge transfer, efficiency, and innovation across the industrial sector.
items_list.content:
As industries face ever-faster technological change, continuous learning has become a critical element of competitiveness. Employees must constantly adapt to new machines, processes, and digital workflows. Traditional training models, however, often fall short—they are time-consuming, costly, and rarely customized to individual needs. Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping this landscape by enabling a new era of dynamic and individualized learning in the workplace.
AI-powered learning systems use data and algorithms to analyze how employees learn, recognize gaps, and personalize content in real time. Instead of one-size-fits-all programs, workers receive guidance and learning materials tailored to their roles and current skill levels. A maintenance technician, for example, might receive instant safety reminders or troubleshooting tutorials directly on a smart device while servicing equipment—based on his previous training data and the task at hand.
Personalization is one of the most powerful benefits of AI in workplace learning. Intelligent platforms identify learning patterns, recommend relevant modules, and adjust to each user’s progress. This increases engagement, shortens learning time, and delivers measurable results. At the organizational level, data analytics make it possible to track workforce skill development and pinpoint specific areas for improvement.
Scalability is another major advantage. Whereas traditional training requires physical sessions and interruptions to the workflow, AI-driven learning tools integrate smoothly into daily operations—accessible anytime via mobile devices, tablets, or AR glasses. For instance, companies like Siemens already use digital learning platforms to deliver adaptive, real-world simulations for apprentices and technical staff.
AI also supports knowledge retention within aging workforces. By capturing expertise and making it digitally accessible, companies can preserve know-how even as experienced staff retire. In this sense, AI becomes not merely a facilitator for learning but a long-term guardian of organizational knowledge.
Ultimately, AI marks the shift from static training to continuous, personalized skill development. For industrial enterprises, embracing AI-driven learning is an investment in resilience and innovation—helping teams learn smarter, faster, and better prepared for the future.

items_list.title: AI and jobs: the real challenge is not technology, but people
items_list.description: AI is rapidly reshaping jobs, increasing demand for new skills and continuous learning. The key challenge is helping people adapt to work alongside these technologies.
items_list.content:
Recent news has once again brought a critical issue into focus: the real impact of artificial intelligence on employment. From layoffs in major tech companies to growing concerns among economists about job polarization, one thing is clear, the labor market is evolving faster than ever.
Some analysts warn that AI could disproportionately affect mid-level roles, potentially reshaping the structure of the workforce and increasing inequality. At the same time, companies are reorganizing their operations, leveraging AI tools to improve efficiency and reduce reliance on traditional roles.
This transformation is not limited to the tech sector. Across Europe, industries are undergoing a deep shift driven by automation, data, and AI adoption. As these technologies become more embedded in daily operations, the demand for new skills is accelerating.
In this context, the real challenge is not whether AI will replace certain tasks, but how we prepare people to work alongside it. Organizations are increasingly recognizing the need for more agile and responsive learning models, ones that are closely connected to the workplace and capable of addressing skills gaps in real time.
At the same time, collaborative initiatives across Europe are exploring how to better align education, training, and industry needs. By bringing together companies, training providers, and technology experts, these ecosystems aim to create more effective and sustainable approaches to workforce development.
Ultimately, AI is not only transforming how we work, but also how we learn. Continuous, work-based, and personalized learning is quickly becoming a strategic priority for organizations that want to remain competitive in a rapidly changing environment.

legal.seo_title: Legal Notice – Privacy & Cookies | Learning Brains
legal.seo_description: Privacy policy, cookie information, and transparency notice of the Learning Brains Erasmus+ project website.
legal.title: Legal
legal.privacy_controller_text: FVEM — Federación Vizcaína de Empresas del Metal, coordinator of the Learning Brains project (Erasmus+ KA220-VET), acts as the data controller for personal data collected through this website.
legal.privacy_data_text: We only collect data that you voluntarily provide through the contact form: name, email address, and message. No data from minors or sensitive data are collected.
legal.privacy_purpose_text: Data is used exclusively to respond to project inquiries and for project dissemination activities. Data is not shared with third parties unless required by law.
legal.privacy_rights_text: You can exercise your rights of access, rectification, erasure, and objection by writing to info@fvem.es. If you consider that the processing is not compliant, you can file a complaint with your national data protection authority.
legal.privacy_legal_basis_text: Data processing is based on the legitimate interest of the consortium for the management and dissemination of the project (Art. 6.1.f GDPR) and on your consent when requested (Art. 6.1.a GDPR).
legal.privacy_retention_text: Data will be kept for the duration of the project (until 2027) and subsequently for the minimum period required by applicable law or audit requirements of the European Commission (usually 5 years after project completion).
legal.privacy_minors_text: This website is not intended for persons under 18 years of age and we do not knowingly collect personal data from minors. If we detect that data from a minor has been collected without parental consent, we will delete it immediately.
legal.cookies_what_text: Cookies are small files stored in your browser to improve the browsing experience. This site uses only technical cookies necessary for the operation of the site.
legal.cookies_types_text: Session cookies: for navigation between pages and the language selector. Google Analytics: to measure traffic anonymously (if enabled). No advertising or third-party tracking cookies are used.
legal.cookies_manage_text: You can disable or delete cookies in your browser settings. Please note that some website functions may stop working correctly if you block all cookies.
legal.cookies_analytics_text: This site may use Google Analytics to measure traffic anonymously. Analytical cookies are only activated if you accept the consent banner. If you decline, no personal data is saved.
legal.eu_disclaimer: Co-funded by the Erasmus+ Programme of the European Union. This project has been funded with support from the European Commission. This publication [communication] reflects the views only of the author, and the Commission cannot be held responsible for any use which may be made of the information contained therein.

cookies_banner.text: We use necessary technical cookies and, if you accept, analytical cookies (Google Analytics) to improve your experience.
